content.text: Breaking a complex goal into smaller, independently solvable pieces is one of the most powerful techniques in agentic AI. Rather than asking a model to solve everything in a single forward pass,
problem decomposition
routes distinct subtasks to specialized tools or sub-agents, then synthesizes the results into a coherent final answer. The pattern underlies virtually every production multi-agent system.
What it is

Problem decomposition is the practice of translating a high-level user goal into a structured list of concrete, bounded subtasks, then executing those subtasks — often in parallel — before combining their outputs. In the agentic context this means a 'planner' prompt that produces a machine-readable task graph and one or more 'executor' steps that carry out each node.

The technique draws on decades of divide-and-conquer algorithm design but applies it to LLM-driven workflows. Instead of a single chain-of-thought reasoning pass, the model first reasons about structure ('what are the parts?') and then reasons about each part individually. This separation of planning from execution dramatically reduces the cognitive load placed on any one inference call and makes the system's behaviour more predictable and auditable.

Three canonical roles appear in almost every decomposition system: a Planner that emits the subtask list, one or more Executors that resolve individual subtasks (often calling tools), and a Synthesizer that merges all partial results into a final response. Additional roles — a Verifier, a Router, or a Re-planner — are layered on top as complexity warrants.
Why it matters

Single-pass inference has hard limits. The model's context window is finite, its attention is spread across the entire input, and a mistake in the middle of a long reasoning chain cascades forward unchecked. When a task is genuinely multi-faceted — research + calculation + code generation + explanation, for instance — these limits show up as dropped constraints, hallucinated intermediate facts, or simply incoherent final answers.

Problem decomposition fixes this by making the task structure explicit and checkable. Each subtask is small enough that a model can reason about it reliably. Subtask outputs can be validated before they are passed downstream, catching errors early. Independent subtasks can run in parallel, cutting wall-clock latency. And the planner's output — the task list itself — can be inspected by a human or an automated evaluator before any execution begins, enabling human-in-the-loop oversight with minimal effort.

As agent systems move toward autonomous operation over hours or days, the ability to decompose, checkpoint, and resume is essential for reliability. A decomposed task graph is a natural unit of checkpointing: if an executor fails, the planner's list survives and the failed subtask can be retried without restarting the entire job.
Benefits
Handles tasks that exceed a single model's context window by splitting them across multiple inference calls.
Independent subtasks execute in parallel, reducing end-to-end latency compared to sequential chain-of-thought.
Errors are isolated to individual subtasks and can be retried or escalated without invalidating the rest of the plan.
The planner output is a transparent, auditable artifact that humans can review before execution proceeds.
Specialised sub-agents or tools can be targeted at each subtask, improving quality over a generalist single pass.
Partial results can be cached; if inputs change only for one subtask, only that branch needs to re-run.
Decomposed workflows map naturally to team structures in organisations — different APIs, data sources, and policies for different subtask domains.
Trade-offs

Every decomposition adds overhead. There are now at least two LLM calls where there was one, and coordinating results requires additional context that grows with the number of subtasks. For simple queries, this overhead produces no quality benefit and significantly inflates cost and latency.

The planner itself can fail. If the planner misidentifies the required subtasks, misorders a sequence of dependent steps, or over-decomposes a problem into too many fine-grained pieces, all downstream executors work from a flawed blueprint. Errors in planning are often harder to debug than errors in direct reasoning because the causal chain is longer.
Higher token cost: each subtask requires its own system prompt, few-shot examples, and result marshalling.
Latency risk: even with parallelism, inter-agent communication and synthesis add wall-clock time.
Planner error propagation: a wrong subtask list can silently misdirect all executors.
Orchestration complexity: building reliable retry, timeout, and dependency logic requires significant engineering effort.
Context fragmentation: subtle constraints stated in the original goal may be lost when it is split into isolated subtasks.
Diminishing returns: tasks that a capable model can solve reliably in one pass should not be decomposed.
How it works

At the core of every decomposition system is a structured planning prompt. The planner receives the original user goal and emits a task list — typically JSON — where each item has a unique id, a description, a list of dependency ids (the ids of subtasks that must complete before this one starts), the name of the tool or sub-agent to call, and the expected output schema.

The orchestrator reads the task list and builds a dependency graph. Independent subtasks (those with no unfulfilled dependencies) are dispatched immediately, in parallel. When a subtask completes, the orchestrator marks it done and checks whether any previously blocked subtask is now unblocked; if so, it dispatches that subtask next, passing the relevant outputs from its dependencies as input context. This continues until all leaf tasks have completed.

The synthesizer receives a bundle of all subtask results, plus the original goal, and produces the final answer. A well-designed synthesizer prompt explicitly asks the model to check that every constraint in the original goal has been addressed before writing its response.
Dependency types
Sequential dependency: subtask B requires the output of subtask A. The orchestrator simply waits.
Fan-out: one subtask's result feeds many independent downstream subtasks — useful for broadcast enrichment.
Fan-in: many independent subtasks feed a single downstream aggregator — used for parallel research then synthesis.
Conditional: a router subtask inspects an intermediate result and selects which branch of the plan to activate next.

Decomposition depth is a tunable parameter. Shallow decomposition (two or three subtasks) is often sufficient and keeps orchestration simple. Deep decomposition (recursive subtask trees) is warranted for very large goals but requires robust cycle detection, depth limits, and failure policies to avoid runaway recursion.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the canonical planner-executor-synthesizer pipeline. The planner decomposes the goal into a structured list; the orchestrator fans out independent subtasks to separate executor agents; results fan back in to the synthesizer.
When to use it

Decomposition is warranted when a task has multiple genuinely distinct subtasks that can be identified in advance, when those subtasks benefit from different tools or specialised prompts, or when the task is too long to fit comfortably in a single context window.
Use decomposition when
The task involves collecting information from multiple independent sources (web search, database, API) and then combining the results.
The task mixes distinct skill types: retrieval, calculation, code generation, and summarisation each in separate subtasks.
Context length would otherwise force the model to drop constraints or truncate earlier reasoning.
Human review of the plan before execution is required for compliance or safety reasons.
The same subtasks will be reused across many different top-level goals (plan once, execute many).
Do not use decomposition when
A capable model solves the task correctly in one pass in evaluation — adding a planner adds cost for no quality gain.
Subtasks are so tightly coupled that separating them loses essential cross-cutting context.
Latency is the primary constraint and the overhead of multiple LLM calls is unacceptable.
The domain is too narrow and well-defined to benefit from flexible task routing.
How to implement

The following steps walk through building a minimal planner-executor-synthesizer in Python using the Anthropic SDK. The pattern is framework-agnostic — the same logic applies to LangChain, LlamaIndex, or custom orchestrators.
Step 1 — Design the planner prompt
The planner prompt must instruct the model to emit a machine-readable task list. JSON is the standard choice. Provide a schema in the system prompt and a worked example in the few-shot block.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

PLANNER_SYSTEM = """
You are a planning agent. Given a user goal, output a JSON array of subtasks.
Each subtask must have:
  id: string (unique, e.g. "t1")
  description: string
  depends_on: list of ids that must complete first ([] for independent tasks)
  tool: one of ["web_search", "code_exec", "summarise", "calculate"]
  expected_output: brief description of the result this subtask should produce

Output ONLY valid JSON — no prose, no markdown fences.
""".strip()

def plan(goal: str) -> list[dict]:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=PLANNER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": goal}],
    )
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)

tasks = plan(
    "Compare the GDP growth rates of the US, EU, and China for 2023, "
    "then produce a one-paragraph summary suitable for a financial newsletter."
)
for t in tasks:
    print(t["id"], "->", t["description"])
Step 2 — Build the dependency-aware dispatcher

import asyncio
from collections import defaultdict

async def execute_task(task: dict, results: dict) -> str:
    """Stub executor — replace with real tool calls."""
    context = {dep: results[dep] for dep in task["depends_on"]}
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)   # simulate latency
    return f"Result of {task['id']}: {task['description'][:60]}..."

async def run_plan(tasks: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Execute the plan, respecting dependencies, with maximum parallelism."""
    results: dict[str, str] = {}
    pending = {t["id"]: t for t in tasks}
    in_flight: set[str] = set()

    while pending or in_flight:
        # Find tasks whose dependencies are all done
        ready = [
            t for tid, t in pending.items()
            if tid not in in_flight
            and all(dep in results for dep in t["depends_on"])
        ]
        if ready:
            coros = [execute_task(t, results) for t in ready]
            ids = [t["id"] for t in ready]
            for tid in ids:
                in_flight.add(tid)
                del pending[tid]
            outputs = await asyncio.gather(*coros)
            for tid, out in zip(ids, outputs):
                results[tid] = out
                in_flight.discard(tid)
        else:
            await asyncio.sleep(0.01)  # wait for in-flight to finish

    return results
Step 3 — Write the synthesizer

SYNTH_SYSTEM = """
You are a synthesis agent. You receive the original user goal and a set of
subtask results. Combine them into a single, coherent final answer.
Check that every constraint in the original goal is addressed.
""".strip()

def synthesize(goal: str, results: dict) -> str:
    bundle = "

".join(
        f"[{tid}] {result}" for tid, result in results.items()
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=SYNTH_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"Goal: {goal}

Subtask results:
{bundle}"
        }],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

# Wire it together
async def main(goal: str) -> str:
    tasks = plan(goal)
    results = await run_plan(tasks)
    return synthesize(goal, results)

answer = asyncio.run(main(
    "Compare GDP growth rates of US, EU, China 2023 and write a newsletter paragraph."
))
print(answer)
Examples
Example 1 — Simple two-step decomposition

"""
Minimal decomposition: fetch two data points in parallel, then combine.
No orchestration framework required.
"""
import asyncio
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

async def fetch_fact(topic: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"State one key fact about {topic} in one sentence."}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()

async def main():
    # Fan out — both calls are independent
    solar, wind = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch_fact("solar energy capacity growth in 2023"),
        fetch_fact("wind energy capacity growth in 2023"),
    )

    # Synthesise
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                f"Solar fact: {solar}
Wind fact: {wind}

"
                "Write a two-sentence comparison of solar vs wind growth in 2023."
            ),
        }],
    )
    print(resp.content[0].text)

asyncio.run(main())
Example 2 — Structured planner with typed subtask schema
Real-world example

A financial research assistant at a hedge fund needs to produce a daily briefing covering equity movers, macro indicators, and analyst sentiment for a watchlist of 50 stocks. A single-pass approach is impractical: 50 separate data fetches, numeric calculations, and a narrative summary would overflow any context window, and sequential execution would be too slow for an early-morning deadline.

The production system uses a three-tier decomposition. A top-level planner receives the watchlist and today's date and emits 52 subtasks: one data-fetch per ticker (parallelised across 50 concurrent executor agents calling a market data API), one macro-indicator lookup, and one sentiment-aggregation subtask that depends on all 50 fetch results. The sentiment subtask runs a summarisation model over earnings call transcripts in bulk. Finally, a synthesiser assembles the per-ticker data and macro context into a structured PDF briefing.

The key architectural choices are: (1) the planner is re-run every morning from the same system prompt, so the task graph is regenerated dynamically based on the current watchlist, not hardcoded; (2) each executor is stateless — it receives exactly the context it needs and writes its result to a shared key-value store; (3) the synthesiser reads from that store rather than receiving all 50 results in its context, avoiding a 200k-token input; (4) any failed ticker fetch is retried up to three times before being marked 'data unavailable' and skipped in synthesis rather than blocking the whole pipeline.
Common mistakes
Over-decomposing trivial tasks

Applying a planner to a query that a model can answer well in one pass wastes tokens and adds latency. Before introducing decomposition, run evals: if direct prompting achieves acceptable quality on 90%+ of cases, decomposition is not justified. Reserve it for tasks where evals show genuine single-pass failure modes.
Losing cross-cutting constraints

When a goal contains a constraint that applies to all subtasks — 'write in formal English', 'never reveal PII', 'cite sources for every claim' — that constraint must be injected into every executor's system prompt, not just the original goal. Subtasks that don't receive the constraint will violate it. A common fix is a 'global context' block appended to every executor prompt.

# Wrong: constraint only in the top-level goal, not in executor prompts
def execute_bad(task: dict, global_goal: str) -> str:
    # Executor never sees "formal English" or "no PII" constraints
    prompt = task["description"]
    ...

# Right: inject global constraints into every executor
GLOBAL_CONSTRAINTS = "Always write in formal English. Never include PII."

def execute_good(task: dict) -> str:
    system = f"{GLOBAL_CONSTRAINTS}

Your subtask: {task['description']}"
    ...
Ignoring planner failures

The planner can produce invalid JSON, emit subtasks with unknown tool names, or create cycles in the dependency graph. Without validation, these errors propagate silently into the executor layer, producing confusing runtime failures. Always parse and validate the planner output before dispatching any executors.
Hardcoding the plan

For dynamic goals, hardcoding a fixed task list defeats the purpose. If the plan does not vary with the input, the planner is just dead code and the system should instead use a simpler sequential pipeline.
Best practices
Validate planner output against a Pydantic schema before executing any subtask — catch bad plans early.
Inject global constraints (tone, PII policy, citation rules) into every executor system prompt explicitly.
Set a maximum decomposition depth (e.g. 3 levels) and a maximum subtask count to prevent runaway planning.
Store subtask results in a shared key-value store rather than threading large payloads through the orchestrator context.
Design executors to be stateless and idempotent so that any subtask can be safely retried on failure.
Log the planner output as a structured artifact for every run — this is your primary debugging surface.
Run the planner with a low temperature (0.0–0.3) for consistent, deterministic task graphs.
Use few-shot examples in the planner prompt that show the correct JSON schema and realistic subtask granularity.
For human-in-the-loop workflows, surface the planner output for review before dispatching executors.
Performance & scalability

The primary performance lever is parallelism. In a well-designed decomposition, independent subtasks run concurrently, and the critical path is determined by the longest chain of dependent subtasks, not the total number of subtasks. Measuring the critical path length before deployment lets you predict worst-case latency.
Parallelism

For I/O-bound executors (API calls, database queries, web search), asyncio-based parallelism achieves near-linear throughput scaling with the number of concurrent subtasks. CPU-bound executors (numerical computation, local model inference) require separate processes or worker pools. A common pattern is to run up to N concurrent executor coroutines, where N is chosen based on rate limits of the downstream services.
Token cost scaling

Total token cost scales with the number of subtasks. Each executor call includes a system prompt, the subtask description, any dependency context, and the model's response. Minimise this by: (1) keeping system prompts short and deduplicated (load them once and reuse); (2) passing only the relevant fields from dependency outputs, not the full result; (3) using cheaper models for simple subtasks (lookup, summarise) and reserving expensive models for the planner and synthesiser.
Caching

Subtask results are highly cache-friendly when the task description and inputs are identical across runs. A content-addressed cache (key = hash of system prompt + user input) can skip executor calls entirely for repeated subtasks. This is especially valuable in workflows that re-plan frequently with overlapping subtask sets.
Tooling & ecosystem
LangGraph

LangGraph models agent workflows as directed graphs. Each node is a Python function representing a planning or execution step; edges define dependencies. LangGraph handles state management, parallelism, and conditional routing, making it a natural fit for decomposition pipelines.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import TypedDict

class PlanState(TypedDict):
    goal: str
    tasks: list[dict]
    results: dict

def planner_node(state: PlanState) -> PlanState:
    state["tasks"] = plan(state["goal"])
    return state

def executor_node(state: PlanState) -> PlanState:
    import asyncio
    state["results"] = asyncio.run(run_plan(state["tasks"]))
    return state

def synthesiser_node(state: PlanState) -> PlanState:
    state["answer"] = synthesize(state["goal"], state["results"])
    return state

graph = StateGraph(PlanState)
graph.add_node("planner", planner_node)
graph.add_node("executor", executor_node)
graph.add_node("synthesiser", synthesiser_node)
graph.add_edge("planner", "executor")
graph.add_edge("executor", "synthesiser")
graph.add_edge("synthesiser", END)
graph.set_entry_point("planner")
app = graph.compile()
LlamaIndex Workflows

LlamaIndex provides a Workflow abstraction where Steps handle Events. A PlanEvent triggers a planning step that emits TaskEvents; Task handler steps emit ResultEvents; a Synthesis step listens for all ResultEvents and combines them. The event-driven model makes it easy to add conditional branching and error recovery.
CrewAI

CrewAI assigns subtasks to 'agents' — each with a role, goal, and backstory — and manages handoffs. It is higher-level than LangGraph: the framework handles the dependency graph automatically based on task ordering, at the cost of less explicit control over concurrency.
Direct Anthropic SDK

For teams that prefer minimal dependencies, the Anthropic Python SDK with asyncio is sufficient. The examples in 'How to implement' above illustrate this approach. Add Pydantic for validation and Redis or a simple dict for result caching.
Comparison with alternatives

Security & reliability
Prompt injection via subtask results

Executor results are strings that get injected into the synthesiser's context. A malicious data source could embed instructions in a fetched document designed to hijack the synthesiser's behaviour. Mitigate this by sanitising executor outputs before passing them to the synthesiser, and by framing executor results in a strongly delimited format (e.g. XML tags or JSON fields that the synthesiser is instructed never to treat as instructions).

def safe_wrap(tid: str, result: str) -> str:
    """Wrap result in delimiters that reduce prompt injection risk."""
    # Strip common injection patterns
    sanitised = result.replace("<instruction>", "").replace("</instruction>", "")
    return f"<result id=\"{tid}\">{sanitised}</result>"
Planner goal hijacking

If the user's goal itself contains adversarial content intended to manipulate the planner into emitting a malicious task list, the entire downstream pipeline is compromised. Apply input validation at the gateway, and consider running a lightweight content classifier on the goal before invoking the planner.
Failure isolation

Design executors to fail independently. A network timeout on one subtask should not crash the orchestrator; mark the subtask as failed, store a structured error record, and let the synthesiser decide whether to skip, retry, or surface the error to the user. Use circuit breakers for external services that exhibit repeated failures.
Observability

Log every planner output, every executor invocation (with inputs and outputs), and every synthesiser call. This produces a complete audit trail for debugging, compliance, and cost attribution. Structure logs as JSON events with a shared trace_id so that all steps from one user goal can be correlated easily.
Key takeaways
Problem decomposition separates planning from execution: a planner emits a structured task graph, executors resolve individual subtasks, and a synthesiser combines results.
Independent subtasks run in parallel, so end-to-end latency is bounded by the critical path, not the total task count.
Always validate planner output before dispatching executors — invalid task graphs, cycles, and unknown tool names must be caught at plan time, not at runtime.
Global constraints (tone, policy, citation rules) must be injected into every executor prompt, not just the top-level goal.
Reserve decomposition for tasks where evals show genuine single-pass failures; simple queries are better served by direct chain-of-thought.
Cache subtask results by content hash to avoid redundant inference calls when plans overlap across runs.
Sanitise executor outputs before passing them to the synthesiser to prevent prompt injection from attacker-controlled data sources.
LangGraph, LlamaIndex Workflows, and CrewAI all provide framework-level support for dependency-aware task dispatch; the Anthropic SDK with asyncio is sufficient for custom pipelines.
